Commercial Mar 25, 2026

"Inexcusable": Why Delta’s CEO Just Stripped Congress Of Their VIP Perks

Lawmakers will have to queue alongside regular passengers.

Delta's removal of congressional flight privileges signals escalating corporate pushback against government relations traditionally leveraged for regulatory influence.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 25, 2026

How The Northrop Grumman’s B-2 Spirit Cockpit Layout Prioritizes Long-Duration Mission Endurance

A closer look at the ergonomics of the world's first fifth-generation bomber.

B-2's cockpit design reduces pilot fatigue through optimized instrument clustering and seat positioning, directly enabling the 15+ hour mission endurance that defines stealth bomber operations.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 25, 2026

Couches In Economy: United Airlines Brings Premium To All Cabins With New Long-Haul Perk

UA's new Relax Row is rolling out in the main cabin this year.

United's Relax Row pricing strategy targets price-sensitive long-haul passengers willing to pay moderately above economy fares, potentially capturing ancillary revenue from the 80% of passengers who don't purchase premium cabin seats.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 25, 2026

Kona Storms Flood Oʻahu

Back-to-back subtropical cyclones in March fueled destructive flash flooding on several of the Hawaiian Islands.

Flash flooding from subtropical cyclones can disrupt airport operations and supply chains across Hawaii, affecting both passenger and cargo aviation serving the islands.

See NASA →

Commercial Mar 25, 2026

How Much Does It Cost To Fly A Boeing 747-400 For 1 Hour?

The Boeing 747-400 is both more expensive and less expensive to operate than modern aircraft with fuel spikes adding to the cost range.

The 747-400's operating costs are highly volatile due to fuel sensitivity, making budgeting unpredictable compared to newer aircraft with more efficient engines.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Mar 25, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation's safety paradigm from investigating accidents after they occur to identifying and mitigating risks before they materialize operationally.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Digitizing borders could free up space to reimagine #PaxEx

Border control and lost baggage are the two remaining “pain points” when it comes to creating seamless air passenger journeys. But smart technology deployment and closer cooperation between airlines, airports and regulators could help resolve these issues and free up enough space to transform the...

Resolving border control inefficiencies through digitization could reclaim physical airport space currently dedicated to security processing, enabling airports to reinvest in passenger experience amenities rather than infrastructure expansion.

See Runway Girl Network →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

How Much Does Pilot Training Cost Before A US Airline First Officer Reaches The Flight Deck?

A closer look at the roadmap to the cockpit.

Aspiring US airline pilots face cumulative training costs exceeding $200,000 before securing a first officer position, creating significant financial barriers to career entry.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 24, 2026

U.S. Considering Deployment of 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East

Senior officials are reportedly considering the deployment of the Immediate Response Force, which includes a Battalion Combat Team of the 82nd Airborne Division, according to U.S. media. After increasing rumors regarding a deployment of U.S. Army troops being in progress, new reports now mention ...

Military airlift capacity will face increased demand if the 82nd Airborne's rapid-response battalion deploys, affecting strategic transport aircraft availability and logistics planning.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Premium For Everyone: United Airlines Invests In 3 Aircraft Types To Transform Flying

Since 2019, the legacy carrier has added nearly 40% more premium seats.

United's diversified premium fleet strategy across aircraft types signals a shift toward capturing higher-margin revenue streams rather than competing primarily on capacity or price.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 25, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Psychological safety and non-punitive reporting are foundational to SMS effectiveness, as they directly influence whether frontline employees surface hazards before incidents occur.

Military Mar 24, 2026

How The Fairchild Republic A-10 Warthog’s Titanium Bathtub Protects Pilots From Ground-Level Ballistics

The story behind America's flying tank buster.

The A-10's titanium armor cockpit design, tested against real combat threats, established a blueprint for survivability engineering that influences modern military aircraft development.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA PC-12 Aircraft Makes Move to Support Flight Research Across Agency

A NASA Pilatus PC-12 aircraft will now be based at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, in order to support flight research efforts across the agency. The PC-12 was acquired in 2022 by NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland for use in advanced technology development....

NASA's PC-12 relocation to Armstrong consolidates flight research capabilities and improves resource efficiency across geographically dispersed research centers.

See NASA →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA

Delta Air Lines said on March 24, 2026, that it is temporarily suspending special services for members of… The post Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA appeared first on AeroTime.

Delta's suspension of Congressional perks signals how extended TSA staffing shortages create operational pressure on carriers to withdraw service differentiation across all passenger segments.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Air Transat Scraps New 12-Hour Longest Route Before A Single Flight Takes Off

The leisure operator was due to take off from Toronto to Ghana's capital on June 27. But the route has been pulled before the first departure...

Leisure carriers face increasing pressure to validate ultra-long routes before launch, suggesting demand forecasting gaps for carrier viability on niche markets.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 25, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Risk Management

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety risk management, which determines the need for, and adequacy of, new or revised risk controls based on the assessment of acceptable risk.

Small operators must systematically assess whether existing risk controls remain adequate or require enhancement as operational conditions and hazards evolve over time.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Business Aviation Mar 24, 2026

How Much Does It Cost To Upgrade To Lie-Flat Business Class On Transatlantic Flights?

Booking business class isn't the only way to access lie-flat transatlantic aviation. Most major transatlantic airlines offer upgrades.

Upgrade pricing strategies vary significantly across carriers, allowing premium economy passengers to access lie-flat beds at substantial savings versus full business class fares.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for satellite execs

A shifting geopolitical landscape is driving huge business opportunities for satcoms but also bringing new supply chain and regulatory challenges, industry executives said March 24 at the Satellite Conference in Washington, D.C. The post Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for s...

Satellite operators must navigate intensifying regulatory fragmentation and supply chain vulnerabilities while capitalizing on geopolitical demand for independent communications infrastructure.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Reminders of Where We’ve Been, Where We’re Going

Three Moon rocks are on display during a March 24, 2026, event where NASA announced a series of transformative agencywide initiatives designed to achieve the National Space Policy and advance American leadership in space. NASA leadership provided updates on mission priorities, including sending t...

NASA's concurrent focus on lunar exploration and broader space leadership initiatives signals a strategic shift toward sustainable deep-space infrastructure rather than isolated missions.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

No Transponder, No Warning: NTSB Details LaGuardia Alert Failure

NTSB says LaGuardia’s surface warning system was "unable to alert."

LaGuardia's ground collision avoidance system failed to function because the aircraft lacked a transponder, revealing a critical gap in airport safety infrastructure that depends on cooperative technologies.

See Simple Flying →

▶15:03
Safety Mar 25, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Assurance

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety assurance, which evaluates the continued effectiveness of implemented risk control strategies and supports the identification of new hazards. Whether you're an owner, operator or part of a small aviation team, this guide provides valuable insights and tools to enhance your safety management practices and ensure a safer operational environment.

Small operators can implement scalable SMS safety assurance practices to continuously validate risk controls and detect emerging hazards without the complexity required of larger carriers.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Expands Swift 100R Engine STC

Amended STC broadens engine coverage for the unleaded avgas.

The expanded STC enables more aircraft models to operate on Swift 100R unleaded fuel, accelerating industry transition away from leaded avgas and reducing fleet fragmentation.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

3 Ways Students Can Get Involved With Artemis

NASA’s Artemis program will establish a sustainable lunar presence, unlock new scientific discoveries, and develop technologies for spaceflight to Mars and beyond – and students can help shape this new era of space exploration. As America launches this new Golden Age of innovation and exploration...

Student participation in Artemis bridges academic engagement with NASA's deep-space infrastructure development, creating a pipeline of talent for sustained lunar operations and Mars exploration missions.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision

The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) held a second briefing, on March 24, 2026, that offered further details… The post NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision appeared first on AeroTime.

Multiple contributing factors rather than a single cause suggests systemic gaps in airport operations, ground control procedures, or pilot-controller communications that may affect other high-density terminals.

See AeroTime Hub →

Industry Mar 24, 2026

NASA Selects Finalists in Student Aircraft Maintenance Competition

Editor’s note: This advisory was updated on March 24, 2026, to update the finalists list. NASA has selected seven student teams as finalists in the 2026 Gateways to Blue Skies Competition, giving them the resources to help address a critical challenge for U.S. aviation: aircraft maintenance. Chal...

NASA's maintenance competition targets the technician workforce shortage by engaging students early, potentially reshaping how the aviation industry recruits skilled mechanics.

See NASA →

Industry Mar 25, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Integrated operational optimization across airlines, airports, and flight systems offers a near-term pathway to accommodate rising demand while reducing environmental impact without waiting for new aircraft technology.

See MDPI →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

ResilienX Receives FAA Waiver for Remote BVLOS Operations

ResilienX, Inc., a leader in aviation safety and BVLOS drone networks, announced today that it

FAA waiver grants ResilienX operational flexibility for beyond-visual-line-of-sight missions, validating commercial viability of autonomous drone networks for infrastructure and emergency response applications.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

When Wireless Networks Falter, Drones Can Provide Backup Connectivity, According to Stevens Researchers

Researchers at Stevens Institute of Technology have developed a drone-based system designed to provide temporary

Drone-delivered backup connectivity could enable emergency responders and isolated communities to maintain critical communications when terrestrial networks fail during disasters.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

America’s new 911 drone is always ready

Drones responding to 911 calls are no longer experimental. Across the US, police departments are already using Drone as First Responder (DFR) programs to get eyes on emergencies faster than patrol units. In many cities, drones arrive in under two minutes, well before officers. But even with that ...

Sub-two-minute drone response times to 911 calls are reshaping emergency dispatch priorities, forcing police departments to fundamentally retriage which incidents warrant immediate aerial assessment versus ground units.

See DroneDJ →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA X-Ray Mission Gets Fresh Look at 2,000-Year-Old Supernova

NASA’s IXPE (Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer) mission has taken a new observation of a supernova, RCW 86, helping fill in a fuller picture of what other telescopes have observed. When astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory previously targeted RCW 86, they discovered that a large “c...

X-ray polarimetry data from IXPE reveals previously unmeasured magnetic field structures in ancient supernova remnants, enabling more precise astrophysical modeling for space-based observatories.

See NASA →

Industry Mar 24, 2026

SparkPNT Enters the Next Chapter with Facet FP

Designed with the belief that users should have total control over the devices they own,

SparkPNT's partnership with Facet FP emphasizes user sovereignty and device autonomy, signaling a shift toward open-architecture positioning systems that reduce dependency on traditional GPS infrastructure.

See sUAS News →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Moog taps Redwire to provide solar arrays for Meteor

WASHINGTON — Redwire announced the first award for its new solar arrays, a $12.8 million contract from Moog. Under the contract announced March 24, Redwire will deliver Extensible Low-Profile Solar Array (ELSA) wings for Moog’s Meteor satellite bus ordered by an undisclosed national security cust...

Redwire's commercial solar array technology is gaining traction in the national security satellite market, signaling growing demand for modular power solutions in military space platforms.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: LaGuardia Surface Alert System Did Not Trigger

Investigators said the fire truck involved did not have a transponder, and a stepped-on radio transmission remains under review.

Equipment gaps and communication failures during emergency response operations can disable airport safety systems designed to prevent collisions, highlighting critical vulnerabilities in ground operations.

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

IAA Prosecutes Drone Operator for Impeding Investigation and Breach of Direction

The Irish Aviation Authority (IAA) brought a prosecution in the District Court against a drone operator for impeding an official investigation

Irish authorities are escalating enforcement by prosecuting obstruction of investigations, signaling stricter accountability for operators who fail to cooperate with regulatory inquiries.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

OrbitsIQ Global Announces Breakthrough in Space-Based IoT Connectivity

Washington, D.C., March 24, 2026 – OrbitsIQ Global (OIQ), an innovator in AI-driven secure connectivity across terrestrial and satellite networks, today announced a major technological milestone achieved in collaboration with […] The post OrbitsIQ Global Announces Breakthrough in Space-Based IoT ...

Space-based IoT connectivity breakthroughs could enable real-time aircraft tracking and operational data collection beyond terrestrial network coverage, expanding aviation's digital infrastructure globally.

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Updates GNSS Interference Guide Months After First Release

Update adds pilot procedures and expands detail on recognizing and reporting jamming and spoofing.

Pilots now have structured procedures to detect and report GNSS attacks, enabling faster industry response to emerging navigation threats.
